text.title: Tool Design Rules, Error Handling, and Security
text.summary: Naming conventions, parameter schema rules, error handling patterns, security guardrails, and antipatterns for production tool use.

rule.statement: Use snake_case action-verb names: get_user_orders, create_ticket, send_email — never generic nouns like orders or data.
rule.rationale: The LLM selects tools by name+description similarity to user intent. Action-verb names reduce ambiguity and improve selection accuracy, especially in dense catalogues.

rule.statement: Write descriptions that state the trigger condition, not just what the tool does: "Use when user asks about weather for a location" beats "Gets weather data".
rule.rationale: LLM tool selection is driven by the description. Explicit trigger phrases reduce false positives (wrong tool called) and false negatives (tool not called when needed).

list.item: One tool, one responsibility — never multiplex unrelated actions into a single tool
Consistent patterns across related tools: similar tools should have similar parameter shapes
Minimize required parameters — sensible defaults reduce LLM argument errors
Include examples in parameter descriptions for enum values and format expectations

rule.statement: Every parameter must have a description field. Never rely on the name alone to communicate meaning.
rule.rationale: LLMs infer parameter intent from the description. Unnamed enum values like ["v1","v2"] are opaque; a description saying "API version: v1=legacy, v2=current" eliminates guessing.

rule.statement: Use additionalProperties: false and strict: true (OpenAI) in production schemas to prevent hallucinated fields.
rule.rationale: Without strict mode, the LLM may add plausible-sounding fields that your code ignores silently, masking errors. Strict mode causes an explicit error instead.

list.item: Use enum for all bounded string values — do not rely on free-text for controlled vocabularies
Never put secrets, connection strings, or credentials in tool definitions — they are sent to the LLM
Keep total tool definitions under 4k tokens to avoid context pressure at high tool-count

rule.statement: Return structured error objects with error_code and message fields — never raise Python exceptions back to the LLM.
rule.rationale: The LLM cannot catch exceptions. A structured {"error": "USER_NOT_FOUND", "message": "..."} lets the model explain the failure and optionally retry with corrected arguments.

example.note: Exception propagation reaches the LLM as an opaque error
example.code: def get_user(user_id: str) -> dict: return db.find(user_id).to_dict() # raises AttributeError on None

rule.statement: Require explicit human confirmation for any destructive or irreversible operation before execution.
rule.rationale: LLMs can be prompted to trigger deletions, payments, or sends through injection attacks or misinterpretation. An out-of-band confirmation gate blocks accidental or malicious execution.

list.item: Validate all LLM-generated arguments against expected types, ranges, and allowed values before execution
Sandbox tool execution environments where possible (no file system access, no shell commands)
Log all tool calls with full arguments and results for audit — include user ID and session ID
Apply rate limiting per tool, per user to protect external APIs
Never expose internal IDs, schema names, or system paths in tool definitions or error messages

antipattern.description: Passing all 50+ available tools to every LLM call.
antipattern.reason: Token cost grows linearly with tool count. At 50 tools, selection accuracy drops significantly. Use RAG-over-tools: embed all tool descriptions, retrieve the top 5–10 by cosine similarity to the user query.

antipattern.description: Accepting free-text from LLM for parameters that should be typed (e.g., user_id as unvalidated string).
antipattern.reason: LLMs hallucinate IDs and enum values. Always validate against a known set or fetch from a prior tool call result; never trust a naked identifier generated from thin air.

antipattern.description: Silent success on tool failure — returning {} or None instead of an error object.
antipattern.reason: The LLM interprets an empty result as success and continues reasoning on missing data, producing confident but wrong answers. Explicit error codes allow the model to stop and report the problem.
A tool returning unexpected output or an LLM stuck in a reasoning loop causes unbounded API calls and cost. Cap at 10–20 iterations and surface a max-iterations error.

text.reference: Registry-based dispatcher implementing all rules above
